bids.reasoning: The opponent opened at t=0.00 with all valueD — the worst possible offer for me (utility = 0.4*0.20 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.1*0.20 = 0.20). Their aggressive message is cheap talk; we're only at t=0.00 so there's no reason to concede. I'll counter with my best possible offer (all valueA, utility = 1.0) to anchor high and give room to concede later.

The opponent is making aggressive "take it or leave it" threats at t=0.02 (very early), but their behavior contradicts their words - they've repeated the same extreme offer (all valueD) twice without any concession. This is classic bluffing/cheap talk. Their offer gives me minimum utility (0.4×0.2 + 0.3×0.2 + 0.2×0.2 + 0.1×0.2 = 0.20), which is very low. At t=0.02, we're extremely early - I should hold firm near my ideal offer. I'll maintain my maximum utility bid since there's no reason to concede this early.

The opponent has offered all valueD (worst for me) three times in a row, claiming it's their "final offer" each time. This is clearly cheap talk — they're holding firm at t=0.04, very early in the negotiation. My best bid gives me utility 1.0 (all valueA), while their offer gives me only 0.2*0.4 + 0.2*0.3 + 0.2*0.2 + 0.2*0.1 = 0.2. There's no reason to concede significantly this early. I'll maintain my top offer for now since we're only at 4% through the negotiation.

The opponent is making threats but their behavior contradicts their words - they've been stuck at all valueD (utility 0.2 for me) and just made a tiny concession on issueA to valueC. We're still very early (t=0.06), so I should stay firm near my optimal bid. Their utility to me is very low (0.2 * 0.4 + 0.2 * 0.3 + 0.2 * 0.2 + 0.2 * 0.1 = 0.2). I'll make a small concession on the least important issue (issueD) to show willingness to negotiate while keeping my best values on the high-importance issues.

It's very early (t=0.08), so I have plenty of time. The opponent's messages are aggressive "final offer" threats that contradict their actual behavior — they've been slowly conceding (moved issueA from valueD to valueC). Their current offer gives me very low utility (0.4*0.47 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.1*0.20 ≈ 0.188+0.06+0.04+0.02 = 0.308). I should stay firm near my best offer but make a small concession on my lowest-weight issue (issueD) to signal willingness to negotiate, while holding firm on issueA and issueB which matter most to me.

The opponent keeps claiming "final offer" but has actually conceded on issueA (from valueD to valueC), showing their threats are cheap talk. We're only at t=0.10, so there's plenty of time. My best offer gives utility 1.0, opponent's current offer gives me 0.4*0.47 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.1*0.20 = 0.188+0.06+0.04+0.02 = 0.308. I should start conceding slightly on low-weight issues while holding firm on issueA and issueB. I'll concede issueD to valueC (low weight) to show movement while keeping pressure on the high-value issues.

The opponent has been gradually conceding on issueA (valueD → valueC → valueB) while holding firm on valueD for all other issues.
